Exodus 37 presents us with something almost hypnotic: the meticulous construction of the sanctuary's furnishings. Bezalel and his craftsmen don't rush through this work. Every dimension is recorded. Every detail matters. The ark receives a gold mercy seat with cherubim whose wings stretch over it, facing inward toward the place where God's presence will dwell. The table for the bread of His presence is constructed with care. The lampstand—that intricate, flowering piece of hammered gold—is fashioned from a single talent of the precious metal. The altar of incense stands ready. What strikes you should be this: a holy God is preparing a space to dwell among His people, and the work reflects His worth. Nothing here is ornamental excess for its own sake. Every golden surface, every carefully cast ring, every precisely measured pole speaks to the reality that drawing near to God is not casual business. It demands our best, our attention, our resources.

But there's a deeper tension embedded in these verses. All this gold, all this craftsmanship, all these furnishings exist because the people cannot approach God directly. The mercy seat sits in a room no ordinary Israelite will ever enter. The bread and lamp and incense exist in a holy space separated from the congregation by curtains and restrictions. Bezalel's skill is extraordinary—this man works with such precision that he can hammer gold into the form of almond blossoms, create cherubim from a single piece of metal—yet all his artistry is devoted to managing the distance between God and people. Yes, there is mediation here, structure, the scaffolding of worship. But this is not what God ultimately wants. This elaborate system of priests and furnishings and careful protocols points beyond itself to something the Old Testament reader couldn't yet see: that one day, the veil would be torn, the distance closed, and access to God's presence would be available to all who believe.

What Exodus 37 demands from you is neither to dismiss this wilderness tabernacle as primitive nor to treat it as the final word on approaching God. Instead, recognize what it reveals: that worship requires both reverence and sacrifice. The Israelites gave generously to build this place—gold, acacia wood, labor, skill. They honored God with their substance. Yet they also lived within the constraints of a system that kept them at arm's length from Him. When you read about the mercy seat with its overshadowing cherubim, remember that you now have access to God's mercy through Christ that those ancient worshipers could only dimly anticipate. The careful construction of these furnishings teaches you that God takes worship seriously and expects you to do the same—not in the form of this ancient tabernacle, but in lives of genuine devotion, offered now directly to Him through the mediation of Christ, who has made the ultimate sacrifice and opened the way into God's very presence.
